name: Hoori
description: Hoori, also known as Hikohohodemi no Mikoto, is a significant figure in Japanese mythology, particularly within the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki accounts of the imperial lineage. He is the youngest of two brothers, the other being Hoderi, and is often associated with the sea and fishing, contrasting with his brother's association with hunting. Hoori's story is a crucial element in legitimizing the divine ancestry of the Japanese imperial family, linking them to the gods and goddesses of the Shinto pantheon. His lineage traces back to Amaterasu, the sun goddess, emphasizing the celestial origins of the ruling dynasty. Hoori's interactions with the sea god Ryujin and his subsequent marriage to Ryujin's daughter, Toyotama-hime, are pivotal events that contribute to the imperial family's perceived authority and right to rule. He is revered as a symbol of prosperity, maritime prowess, and the divine connection between the land and the sea.
subtitle: The Fire Subduing Prince

keyMyths.title: The Lost Fishing Hook
keyMyths.description: Hoori borrowed his elder brother Hoderi's fishing hook, but unfortunately lost it in the sea. Hoderi demanded its return, leading to a conflict between the brothers. Hoori searched tirelessly but could not find the hook. Distraught, he sought help from the sea god, Ryujin, who offered him assistance. This myth highlights the importance of fulfilling obligations and the consequences of carelessness, while also demonstrating Hoori's perseverance and eventual good fortune.

keyMyths.title: Marriage to Toyotama-hime
keyMyths.description: Ryujin helped Hoori find the lost fishing hook and, furthermore, offered his daughter, Toyotama-hime, in marriage. Hoori and Toyotama-hime lived happily in the sea palace for several years. However, Hoori eventually longed to return to his homeland. Toyotama-hime agreed to accompany him, but she requested that he not watch her give birth. Curiosity got the better of Hoori, and he broke his promise, leading to Toyotama-hime's departure back to the sea.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Ugayafukiaezu
keyMyths.description: Toyotama-hime, ashamed that Hoori witnessed her birth in her true form (a dragon or shark), returned to the sea after giving birth to Ugayafukiaezu. She entrusted her sister, Tamayori-hime, to raise the child. Ugayafukiaezu later married Tamayori-hime, and they had a son named Jimmu, who is considered the first Emperor of Japan. This myth establishes the divine lineage of the imperial family and their connection to both the land and the sea.
